did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computational methods including k-means clustering for segmentation and the extraction of shape (eccentricity, metric) and texture (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity) features from dermoscopy images to identify skin cancer types. These techniques constitute machine learning and computer vision approaches applied to dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the extraction of shape and texture features from 60 dermoscopy images to identify skin cancer types. It details specific methods (RGB to LAB conversion, k-means clustering segmentation, feature extraction using Matlab), presents results in tables and figures, and includes a discussion of these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Extraction of Shape and Texture Features of Dermoscopy Image for Skin Cancer Identification
journal: Sinkron : Jurnal dan Penelitian Teknik Informatika
brief_summary: This study aimed to identify six types of skin cancer (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion) using dermoscopy images. The researchers utilized a dataset of 60 images obtained from Kaggle, processing them by converting RGB to LAB color space and segmenting lesions using k-means clustering. Shape features (eccentricity, metric) and texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity) were extracted from the segmented images. The identification was performed using a custom program developed in the Matlab application. The results demonstrated that the technique of extracting shape and texture features effectively identifies skin cancer types, though the authors suggest using larger datasets and adding color features in future work.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer type (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The study aims to identify the type of skin cancer from 6 discrete categories using extracted shape and texture features. The process involves feature extraction followed by identification/classification using a custom Matlab program. The dataset consists of 60 images across 6 classes (10 images per class).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Dermoscopy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 60 dermoscopy images obtained from the Kaggle site, consisting of 6 categories of skin diseases/cancers.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and identification via a custom Matlab program; specific training/validation/testing splits were not reported.
use.testing: Used for identification/testing via a custom Matlab program; specific training/validation/testing splits were not reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study describes a method for feature extraction and identification using a custom Matlab program and claims the technique works well, but provides no statistical validation of this claim.

Kaggle Dermoscopy Dataset.metric: Qualitative identification success; specific feature values for one test case

Custom Matlab Program (using k-means seg.identification_result: Successfully identified skin cancer types (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Vascular Lesion) based on extracted features.

Kaggle Dermoscopy Dataset.notes: The paper states the technique 'works well' and 'proven to work well' in identifying skin cancer types. No quantitative accuracy percentage, confusion matrix, or statistical significance (p-value) is reported for the overall dataset. Feature values for all 60 images are presented in Table 4, but specific classification accuracy metrics are not provided.

preprocessing_techniques: Median filter (de-noising technique applied to enhance image quality and maintain edges)
RGB to LAB color space conversion (transforming Red, Green, Blue channels to Lightness, A, B components to separate brightness and color information)
conclusions: The technique of extracting shape features (eccentricity, metric) and texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity) from dermoscopy images is proven to work well in identifying the type of skin cancer.

The proposed method successfully identified six types of skin cancer (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion) using a custom program developed in the Matlab application.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the small dataset size (60 images) and the exclusion of color features, suggesting that future work should use more data and add color features to improve identification.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 60 dermoscopy images (10 per class) sourced from Kaggle, which severely limits statistical power and generalizability.

No quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, confusion matrix) are reported; the authors claim the technique 'works well' based on qualitative identification of a single test case.

The paper lacks external validation, cross-validation, or a held-out test set; the 'identification' process appears to be a demonstration of feature extraction and database lookup rather than a trained classifier evaluated on unseen data.
There is no statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals reported to support the claim that the extracted features effectively distinguish between the six skin cancer types.
The methodology excludes color features despite their known importance in dermoscopy, and the authors themselves acknowledge this as a limitation, suggesting future work should add color features.

The classification mechanism is not clearly defined as a supervised learning algorithm but rather as a rule-based or database-matching system using a custom Matlab program, making reproducibility and comparison with standard ML/DL benchmarks difficult.

The dataset composition includes benign lesions (Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis) alongside malignant ones (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma), but the small sample size per class prevents robust analysis of inter-class separability.
